schools.summary: The making of the altar of burnt offering: 'and thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood, five cubits long, and five cubits broad; the altar shall be foursquare: and the height thereof shall be three cubits' — the altar of burnt offering (of acacia wood, overlaid with brass, vv.2), foursquare, of the given dimensions, in the court (the great altar where the sacrifices were offered, the brazen altar, the first object on entering the court, the place of atonement and approach to God); the altar of sacrifice — the place where the burnt offerings and sacrifices were offered (the daily sacrifices, the sin offerings, the burnt offerings, Leviticus 1-7, the witnesses noting the figure — the altar of burnt offering a figure of Christ and his cross, the place of sacrifice and atonement, where the offering was made, the blood shed, the wrath appeased, the lesson — Christ both the altar and the sacrifice, Hebrews 13:10, 'we have an altar,' the cross the altar, Christ the offering, Ephesians 5:2, 'an offering and a sacrifice to God,' the way to God through sacrifice, the sinner approaching God first at the altar, the blood, the atonement, before the laver, the holy place, the holiest, the order of approach, through the cross); the altar foursquare (the great altar of the court) — the place of the daily sacrifice (the providence, the altar the figure of the cross, the place of atonement, the way to God, the first thing in the court, the sacrifice the ground of all approach, Hebrews 9:22, 'without shedding of blood is no remission') — Henry, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the altar of burnt offering signify?

sides.position: The place of sacrifice and atonement (Leviticus 1-7), a figure of Christ and his cross (Hebrews 13:10, 'we have an altar'; Ephesians 5:2) - the way to God through sacrifice, the first thing in the court, the atoning sacrifice the ground of all approach (Hebrews 9:22)

themes: And thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood, five cubits long, and five cubits broad; the altar shall be foursquare: and the height thereof shall be three cubits
the making of the altar of burnt offering (the crux of the place of sacrifice)
the place of sacrifice and atonement, the first thing in the court (Leviticus 1-7)
a figure of Christ and his cross, the way to God through sacrifice (Hebrews 13:10; Ephesians 5:2; 9:22)

schools.summary: The horns of the altar and the overlay of brass: 'and thou shalt make the horns of it upon the four corners thereof: his horns shall be of the same: and thou shalt overlay it with brass' — the horns — 'the horns of it upon the four corners... his horns shall be of the same': four horns (projections) at the four corners of the altar, of one piece with it (the witnesses noting the uses and figure — the horns where the blood of the sin offering was applied, Leviticus 4:7, 18, 25, 30, the atoning blood put on the horns, the horns laid hold of for refuge, the place of asylum, Adonijah and Joab fleeing to 'the horns of the altar,' 1 Kings 1:50-51; 2:28, the horns to which the sacrifice was bound, Psalm 118:27, 'bind the sacrifice with cords, even unto the horns of the altar,' the lesson — the horns of the altar the place of refuge and the application of the blood, a figure of Christ the refuge and the atonement, the sinner laying hold on Christ and the horns of his altar, fleeing for refuge to the hope set before us, Hebrews 6:18, the power and salvation of the cross, the 'horn of salvation,' Luke 1:69, the strength and refuge of the atonement); the horns of one piece with the altar (not separate, the horns and the altar one) — the unity (the horns part of the altar, the refuge part of the sacrifice); 'thou shalt overlay it with brass' (the altar overlaid with brass, bronze, able to bear the fire, the figure, some note, of judgment endured, the brazen altar bearing the fire of God's wrath, as Christ bore the fire of judgment, the providence, the brazen altar, the place of atonement, the fire of judgment borne, a figure of the cross, where Christ endured the fire of wrath) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What do the horns of the altar signify?

sides.position: The place where the atoning blood was applied (Leviticus 4:7) and where the fleeing laid hold for refuge (1 Kings 1:50-51; Psalm 118:27) - a figure of Christ the refuge and the atonement (Hebrews 6:18; Luke 1:69, the 'horn of salvation'); the brass bearing the fire of judgment, a figure of the cross

themes: And thou shalt make the horns of it upon the four corners thereof: his horns shall be of the same: and thou shalt overlay it with brass
the horns of the altar and the overlay of brass (the crux of the horns of refuge)
the place where the atoning blood was applied and the fleeing laid hold for refuge (Leviticus 4:7; 1 Kings 1:50-51; Psalm 118:27)
a figure of Christ the refuge and the atonement (Hebrews 6:18; Luke 1:69), the brass bearing the fire of judgment

schools.summary: The brazen vessels of the altar: 'and thou shalt make his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons, and his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass' — the vessels of the altar (pans for the ashes, shovels, basins for the blood, fleshhooks for the flesh, firepans for the coals) all of brass (the furniture of the altar, the instruments for the service of the sacrifices, the brass, the vessels of the altar, matching the brazen altar, for the service of atonement); the pans, shovels, basins (for the ashes, the cleansing, the blood) — the instruments of the altar's service; 'all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass' (the fleshhooks, for the flesh of the sacrifices, and firepans, for the coals, all of brass, the providence, the vessels of the altar all brass, for the service of the sacrifices, the daily atonement) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons, and his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass
the brazen vessels of the altar
the instruments for the service of the sacrifices
the daily atonement served by the vessels of brass

schools.summary: The brazen grate of the altar: 'and thou shalt make for it a grate of network of brass; and upon the net shalt thou make four brasen rings in the four corners thereof' — a grate (a grating, a network) of brass made for the altar, with four brazen rings at its corners (for the staves, vv.6-7, the grate, the bronze network, perhaps a hearth or ledge for the fire and the offerings, or a grating in the middle of the altar, the rings, for the staves to bear the altar, the workmanship of the altar); the brazen grate (the network, for the fire and the offerings, set in the altar) — the hearth (the workmanship); 'four brasen rings in the four corners thereof' (the rings, for the staves, the carrying of the altar, vv.6-7, the provision for bearing, the altar borne by staves, the providence, the altar carried in the journey, the pilgrim sanctuary) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt make for it a grate of network of brass; and upon the net shalt thou make four brasen rings in the four corners thereof
the brazen grate of the altar
the bronze network for the fire and the offerings
the rings for the staves to bear the altar (vv.6-7)

schools.summary: The placing of the grate within the altar: 'and thou shalt put it under the compass of the altar beneath, that the net may be even to the midst of the altar' — the grate placed under the ledge (compass, a projecting rim) of the altar, reaching to the middle of the altar's height (the placement, the grate set within, halfway up the altar, perhaps to hold the fire and the offerings, or as a grating through which the ashes fell, the workmanship, the grate fitted within the altar); the compass (a ledge, a projecting border around the altar) under which the grate was set — the placement (the structure); 'that the net may be even to the midst of the altar' (the grate reaching to the middle, halfway up the altar's height, the workmanship, the grate fitted within, the providence, the altar made as appointed, the grate for the fire and the service) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt put it under the compass of the altar beneath, that the net may be even to the midst of the altar
the placing of the grate within the altar
the bronze network fitted within, halfway up the altar
for the fire and the service of the altar

schools.summary: The staves for bearing the altar: 'and thou shalt make staves for the altar, staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with brass' — the staves (poles, of acacia wood overlaid with brass, matching the brazen altar) for carrying the altar (the staves, for the bearing of the altar, through the rings, vv.7, the brass overlay, matching the altar, not gold as the ark's, for the altar was brass, the provision for transport, the altar borne by staves); the staves of acacia (for carrying the altar) — the poles (the bearing); 'overlay them with brass' (the brass overlay, matching the altar of the court, the brazen altar, the provision, the staves through the rings, the altar borne, vv.7, the providence, the altar carried in the journey, the pilgrim sanctuary) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt make staves for the altar, staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with brass
the staves for bearing the altar
the poles of acacia overlaid with brass, matching the brazen altar
the provision for carrying the altar in the journey

schools.summary: The staves placed to bear the altar: 'and the staves shall be put into the rings, and the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it' — the staves put through the rings on the two sides of the altar, that it might be carried (the bearing, the altar borne by the staves, on the journey, the provision for transport, the altar, like the ark and table, carried by staves, the pilgrim sanctuary, the altar ever ready to move with the people); the staves through the rings (the altar made ready to bear) — the provision (the bearing); 'the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it' (the staves on the two sides, for the bearing, the altar carried by the staves, the providence, the altar borne in the journey, the furniture carried by the Levites, Numbers 4:13-14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the staves shall be put into the rings, and the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it
the staves placed to bear the altar
the altar carried by the staves in the journey (Numbers 4:13-14)
the pilgrim sanctuary ever ready to move

schools.summary: The altar made hollow according to the pattern: 'hollow with boards shalt thou make it: as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it' — the altar made hollow (an empty box of boards, not solid), perhaps to be filled with earth or stones, or left hollow for the fire (the hollow altar, light to carry, the boards of acacia overlaid with brass, the figure, some note, the altar hollow, to be filled, perhaps as the cross bears the offering); the altar hollow (boards, not solid, perhaps filled with earth, Exodus 20:24) — the structure (the lightness for carrying); 'as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it' (the altar made according to the pattern shown in the mount, as all the furniture, Exodus 25:40; Hebrews 8:5, the exactness, the altar by the divine model, the providence, the altar a copy of the heavenly pattern, the worship by God's appointment, Hebrews 8:5) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Hollow with boards shalt thou make it: as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it
the altar made hollow according to the pattern
the hollow altar of boards, light to carry (Exodus 20:24)
made by the heavenly pattern, the worship by God's appointment (Exodus 25:40; Hebrews 8:5)

schools.summary: The making of the court of the tabernacle: 'and thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle: for the south side southward there shall be hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side' — the court (the enclosed outer space around the tabernacle, bounded by hangings of fine twined linen on pillars, the open enclosure, where the altar and laver stood, where the people brought their offerings, the hangings, the white linen walls, a hundred cubits long on the south side); the court (the enclosure around the tabernacle, the outer area, the place of the altar and laver) — the figure (some note, the court the visible church, or the outer court of God's house, the place of access to the altar); 'hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side' (the white linen hangings, the walls of the court, the figure of purity and righteousness, Revelation 19:8, the enclosure, separating the holy court from the world, the providence, the court the place of approach, the white linen the righteousness required, the figure of the holiness of God's house) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle... hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side
the making of the court of the tabernacle
the white linen walls enclosing the holy court, the place of approach to the altar
the figure of purity and righteousness (Revelation 19:8)

schools.summary: The pillars, sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court: 'and the twenty pillars thereof and their twenty sockets shall be of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver' — the hangings of the court hung on twenty pillars (for the south side) set in twenty brazen sockets, with silver hooks and fillets (connecting-rods or bands, the pillars and sockets of brass, the supports of the court, the hooks and fillets of silver, the connecting parts, the silver bands, the structure of the court); the pillars and sockets of brass (the supports of the hangings, the brazen bases) — the structure (the strength); 'the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver' (the hooks and fillets of silver, the connecting parts, the silver bands joining the pillars, the gradation of metals, brass for the court, silver for the connections, gold for the holiest, the providence, the court of brass and silver, the gradation of glory from the court to the holiest) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the twenty pillars thereof and their twenty sockets shall be of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver
the pillars, sockets, hooks, and fillets of the court
the supports of the court - pillars and sockets of brass, hooks and fillets of silver
the gradation of metals from the court to the holiest

schools.summary: The hangings of the north side of the court: 'and likewise for the north side in length there shall be hangings of an hundred cubits long, and his twenty pillars and their twenty sockets of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver' — the north side, like the south (vv.9-10), having hangings of a hundred cubits on twenty pillars and twenty brazen sockets, with silver hooks and fillets (the symmetry, the two long sides matching, the enclosure, the white linen walls on the north as on the south); the north side matching the south (the hangings, the pillars, the sockets) — the symmetry (the enclosure); 'twenty pillars and their twenty sockets of brass; the hooks... and their fillets of silver' (the same materials as the south, brass and silver, the structure, the court's long sides, the providence, the court enclosed on both long sides, the symmetry of God's house) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And likewise for the north side in length there shall be hangings of an hundred cubits long, and his twenty pillars and their twenty sockets of brass...
the hangings of the north side of the court
the north side matching the south (vv.9-10)
the white linen walls enclosing the court on both long sides

schools.summary: The hangings of the west side of the court: 'and for the breadth of the court on the west side shall be hangings of fifty cubits: their pillars ten, and their sockets ten' — the west side (the breadth, the back of the court) having hangings of fifty cubits on ten pillars and ten sockets (the west side, the shorter end, fifty cubits, half the length of the long sides, the enclosure, the white linen on the west, the back of the court closed); the west side fifty cubits (the breadth, half the length) — the proportion (the enclosure of the back); 'their pillars ten, and their sockets ten' (the ten pillars and sockets, for the west side, the structure, the back of the court enclosed, the providence, the court enclosed on the west, the proportions of God's house) — Gill.

themes: And for the breadth of the court on the west side shall be hangings of fifty cubits: their pillars ten, and their sockets ten
the hangings of the west side of the court
the west side fifty cubits, half the length of the long sides
the white linen enclosing the back of the court

schools.summary: The breadth of the east side, the entrance: 'and the breadth of the court on the east side eastward shall be fifty cubits' — the east side (the front, the entrance, eastward) fifty cubits broad (like the west, the east side, the front of the court, where the gate was, vv.16, the entrance, the court entered from the east, the one way in, the orientation, the tabernacle facing east, the entrance toward the sunrise); the east side (the front, the entrance) — the orientation (the front of the court); 'shall be fifty cubits' (the fifty cubits, the breadth of the east side, matching the west, the entrance side, where the gate was, vv.16, the providence, the court entered from the east, the one gate, the figure of the one way in, vv.16; John 10:9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the breadth of the court on the east side eastward shall be fifty cubits
the breadth of the east side, the entrance
the front of the court, where the gate was (vv.16)
the court entered from the east, the one way in (John 10:9)

schools.summary: The hangings on one side of the gate: 'the hangings of one side of the gate shall be fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three' — on one side of the gate (the entrance), hangings of fifteen cubits on three pillars and three sockets (the gate-side hangings, the white linen flanking the gate, the structure, the front partly enclosed by hangings, the gate in the middle, vv.16, the entrance, the gate flanked by the linen hangings); the fifteen cubits on one side of the gate (the white linen flanking the entrance) — the structure (the partial enclosure); 'their pillars three, and their sockets three' (the three pillars and sockets, for the gate-side hangings, the structure, the front enclosed except for the gate, the providence, the gate flanked by the hangings, the one entrance in the middle, vv.16) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The hangings of one side of the gate shall be fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three
the hangings on one side of the gate
the white linen flanking the gate, the front partly enclosed
the one entrance in the middle (vv.16)

schools.summary: The hangings on the other side of the gate: 'and on the other side shall be hangings fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three' — on the other side of the gate, likewise hangings of fifteen cubits on three pillars and three sockets (the symmetry, the two sides of the gate matching, fifteen cubits each, with the gate of twenty cubits between, vv.16, the front enclosed, except for the gate in the middle, the one entrance); the other side matching (fifteen cubits, the white linen flanking the gate) — the symmetry (the enclosure); 'their pillars three, and their sockets three' (the three pillars and sockets, matching the other side, the structure, the front enclosed on both sides of the gate, the providence, the gate flanked symmetrically, the one entrance in the middle, vv.16) — Gill.

themes: And on the other side shall be hangings fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three
the hangings on the other side of the gate
the front enclosed symmetrically on both sides of the gate
the one entrance in the middle (vv.16)

schools.summary: The gate of the court: 'and for the gate of the court shall be an hanging of twenty cubits, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework: and their pillars shall be four, and their sockets four' — the gate of the court (the one entrance, twenty cubits wide, in the middle of the east side) a hanging of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework, on four pillars (the gate, the one entrance into the court, richer than the plain white hangings, the colored gate, the entrance, the one way into the court, where the altar stood); the figure — the gate the one entrance into the court (the only way in, twenty cubits wide, ample, yet one, the witnesses noting the lesson — the one gate a figure of Christ the one way to God, the only entrance, 'I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved,' John 10:9; 'I am the way... no man cometh unto the Father, but by me,' John 14:6, the one entrance, no other way in, the gate wide enough for all who come, yet the one and only way, the colors, blue, purple, scarlet, white, the figure of Christ, the heavenly, kingly, sacrificial, and pure, the one way of access, through Christ alone, the gate of the court the figure of the way to God); 'their pillars shall be four, and their sockets four' (the four pillars of the gate, for the colored hanging, the structure, the gate hung on four pillars, the providence, the one gate, the one way in, the figure of Christ the door) — Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the gate of the court signify?

sides.position: The one and only entrance into the court (John 10:9; 14:6), a figure of Christ the one way to God - the only door, wide enough for all who come yet the one entrance; the colors (blue, purple, scarlet, white) the figure of Christ (heavenly, kingly, sacrificial, pure)

themes: And for the gate of the court shall be an hanging of twenty cubits, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework
the gate of the court (the crux of the one entrance)
the one and only way into the court, wide enough for all who come
a figure of Christ the one door and way to God (John 10:9; 14:6)

schools.summary: The silver and brass of the court's pillars: 'all the pillars round about the court shall be filleted with silver; their hooks shall be of silver, and their sockets of brass' — all the pillars of the court banded (filleted) with silver, with silver hooks, and brass sockets (the silver, the bands and hooks, the connecting silver, the figure of redemption, the silver of the atonement-money, Exodus 30:11-16, the brass, the sockets, the bases, the figure of strength or judgment, the structure of the court); the silver fillets and hooks (the silver bands joining the pillars) — the connecting silver (the figure, the silver of redemption, the atonement-money, Exodus 30:11-16, binding the court together); 'their sockets of brass' (the brazen sockets, the bases of the pillars, standing on brass, the structure, the court resting on brass, joined with silver, the providence, the court of brass and silver, the gradation of metals, the figure of redemption and strength) — JFB, Gill.

themes: All the pillars round about the court shall be filleted with silver; their hooks shall be of silver, and their sockets of brass
the silver and brass of the court's pillars
the court joined with the silver of redemption (the atonement-money, Exodus 30:11-16)
resting on brass, the gradation of metals

schools.summary: The dimensions of the court: 'the length of the court shall be an hundred cubits, and the breadth fifty every where, and the height five cubits of fine twined linen, and their sockets of brass' — the court a hundred cubits long, fifty broad, and the hangings five cubits high, of fine twined linen, on brazen sockets (the dimensions, the court a long rectangle, twice as long as broad, the height, the linen walls five cubits high, enclosing the court, the brass sockets, the bases); the hundred by fifty cubits (the court's proportions) — the rectangle (the enclosure); 'the height five cubits of fine twined linen, and their sockets of brass' (the linen walls five cubits high, enclosing the court, screening it from without, the brass sockets, the bases, the providence, the court enclosed, the white linen walls, the figure of the holiness and separation of God's house) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The length of the court shall be an hundred cubits, and the breadth fifty every where, and the height five cubits of fine twined linen, and their sockets of brass
the dimensions of the court
the court a hundred by fifty cubits, the walls five cubits high
the white linen walls enclosing the court, the holiness and separation of God's house

schools.summary: The brazen vessels and pins of the tabernacle: 'all the vessels of the tabernacle in all the service thereof, and all the pins thereof, and all the pins of the court, shall be of brass' — all the vessels (instruments) for the service of the tabernacle, and all the pins (pegs, stakes) of the tabernacle and the court, of brass (the vessels, for the service, the daily work, the pins, the tent-pegs, fastening the curtains and hangings, holding the structure firm, the brass, the common metal for the service and the fastenings); the vessels for the service (the instruments of the daily work) — the furniture of service; 'all the pins thereof, and all the pins of the court, shall be of brass' (the pins, the tent-pegs, the stakes, holding the curtains and hangings firm against the wind, the brass, the common, strong metal, the providence, the tabernacle held firm by the pins, the figure, some note, of the small but necessary parts that hold God's house together, every member needful, 1 Corinthians 12:22) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: All the vessels of the tabernacle in all the service thereof, and all the pins thereof, and all the pins of the court, shall be of brass
the brazen vessels and pins of the tabernacle
the instruments for the service and the pins holding the structure firm
every part needful, the small parts that hold God's house together (1 Corinthians 12:22)

schools.summary: The pure oil for the light that must burn always: 'and thou shalt command the children of Israel, that they bring thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamp to burn always' — the oil — 'pure oil olive beaten for the light': the people to bring pure olive oil, beaten (pressed from the olives, the finest, clearest oil), for the light of the lampstand (the witnesses noting the figure — the pure beaten oil, the finest, the purest, for the holy light, the oil a figure of the Spirit, the oil of the Spirit, the anointing, that feeds the light of testimony and grace, Zechariah 4:2-6, 'not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit'; 1 John 2:20, 27, the anointing, the beaten oil, the finest, pressed, some note, the oil pressed, as Christ was bruised, or the saints tried, that the light might burn, the costly purity of the oil for the holy light); 'to cause the lamp to burn always' (the lamp to burn continually, always, every night, perpetually, the light never going out, Leviticus 24:2-4, the continual light of the sanctuary, the figure, the light of testimony, the church, the word, ever burning, never extinguished, Matthew 5:14-16, the lamp of God, 1 Samuel 3:3, the light that shines in the darkness, John 1:5, the perpetual witness); the continual light — the pure oil feeding the perpetual lamp (the providence, the light of the sanctuary ever burning, the figure of the Spirit's light and the perpetual testimony of the church, fed by the pure oil of the Spirit) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does the pure oil and the continual light signify?

sides.position: The finest, purest oil for the holy light a figure of the Spirit's anointing that feeds the light (Zechariah 4:2-6; 1 John 2:20, 27), and the lamp burning continually (Leviticus 24:2-4) the perpetual light of the sanctuary - a figure of the unceasing testimony of the church and the light that shines in the darkness (Matthew 5:14-16; John 1:5)

themes: And thou shalt command the children of Israel, that they bring thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamp to burn always
the pure oil for the light that must burn always (the crux of the continual light)
the pure beaten oil a figure of the Spirit's anointing that feeds the light (Zechariah 4:2-6; 1 John 2:20)
the lamp burning continually, the perpetual testimony of the church (Leviticus 24:2-4; Matthew 5:14-16; John 1:5)

schools.summary: The priests tending the lamp before the LORD continually: 'in the tabernacle of the congregation without the vail, which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD: it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations on the behalf of the children of Israel' — in the holy place (outside the veil, before the ark of the testimony), Aaron and his sons to tend (order, trim, keep) the lamp from evening to morning before the LORD (the place, the holy place, where the lampstand stood, the priestly office, the priests tending the light, the daily service, the continual light, kept burning through the night, before the LORD); the priests ordering the lamp (trimming, dressing, keeping it burning, evening to morning) — the priestly service (the daily tending of the light, the figure, the ministers keeping the light of testimony burning, the church's light tended); 'it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations on the behalf of the children of Israel' (the perpetual statute, the tending of the light a lasting ordinance, for all generations, the figure, the perpetual light, the perpetual ministry, the light of testimony kept burning in every generation, the providence, the continual light of the sanctuary, the perpetual ordinance, the figure of the unceasing light of the church and the Spirit, Revelation 1:20, the light ever before God on behalf of the people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: In the tabernacle of the congregation without the vail, which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD: it shall be a statute for ever
the priests tending the lamp before the LORD continually
the priests keeping the light of the sanctuary burning, evening to morning
the perpetual ordinance, a figure of the unceasing light of the church (Revelation 1:20)
